topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights caught the canvas, transforming the raw pigment into something incandescent. I stood near the entrance, my breath catching in my throat, unable to move. It was *my* work—the series I’d labored over for three years, the very soul of my recent explorations—and it hung there, framed perfectly, surrounded by other pieces I didn't recognize. A strange, dizzying feeling washed over me, a complete disorientation. I took a hesitant step forward, my gaze tracing the intricate lines, the sudden intensity of the color vibrating against my vision. My hands instinctively clenched at my sides, the air seeming too thin to draw in. "This... this is extraordinary," I managed to whisper, the sound barely escaping my lips, my eyes wide, taking in the sheer scale of the presentation.

[story 2]

I found myself leaning against a cold marble pillar, watching the crowd mill around the unveiled exhibition. Then, my eyes snagged on it. My signature piece, the one I’d fought so hard to finish, was dominating the center wall. The way the light fractured across the texture, the depth of the shadows—it stopped my heart. A tremor ran through my arms, making my fingers twitch. I edged closer, drawn by an almost physical pull toward the surface. I couldn't look away; my focus narrowed entirely onto the brushstrokes, a silent, profound recognition blooming in my chest. I swallowed hard, my mouth suddenly dry, and I found myself simply staring, unable to articulate the sheer impact the sight had on my entire being.

[story 3]

The hushed reverence of the room pressed in on me. I had come expecting a quiet display, a respectful nod to my recent efforts. Instead, the room was flooded with the presence of my art, presented as if it belonged to some other master. When my eyes landed on the large oil painting, a sharp intake of breath escaped me. It wasn't just the skill; it was the feeling it evoked—a sudden, overwhelming sense of belonging and recognition that made my knees feel unsteady. I took a slow, deliberate walk toward it, my steps feeling heavy and slow. I stopped just short of the barrier, my gaze fixed, my pupils dilating as I tried to process the sheer, breathtaking audacity of the display.
